The Employer shall provide reasonable notice of material changes to policies. Breach of a material policy may constitute cause for termination.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Lease in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: The Landlord hereby leases to the Tenant the Premises described in Schedule A for a term of five (5) years commencing on the Commencement Date. The Tenant shall have the option to renew for two (2) additional terms of five (5) years each, provided the Tenant gives written notice of its intention to renew not less than six (6) months prior to the expiry of the then-current term and is not in default under this Lease at the time of such notice.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Lease in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: In addition to Base Rent, the Tenant shall pay as Additional Rent its Proportionate Share of all Operating Costs, realty taxes, and insurance premiums for the Building, calculated annually and payable in equal monthly installments. Operating Costs include all costs incurred by the Landlord in operating, maintaining, repairing, and managing the Building and common areas. The Landlord shall provide an annual reconciliation statement within ninety (90) days after each fiscal year.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Lease in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: The Tenant may make improvements, alterations, or additions to the Premises only with the prior written consent of the Landlord, which shall not be unreasonably withheld. All improvements shall be performed by licensed contractors with appropriate insurance, in compliance with the Ontario Building Code and all applicable laws. Leasehold improvements shall become the property of the Landlord upon installation unless otherwise agreed in writing.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Purchase Sale in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: The Buyer agrees to purchase from the Seller the property municipally known as [Address] (the 'Property') for the Purchase Price, subject to the terms and conditions herein. The Buyer shall have until the Requisition Date to examine title and satisfy itself that there are no outstanding work orders or deficiency notices affecting the Property. If the Buyer is not satisfied, in its sole and absolute discretion, the Buyer may terminate this Agreement and the deposit shall be returned in full.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Purchase Sale in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: The Seller represents and warrants that: (a) the Seller has good and marketable title to the Property free from all encumbrances except as disclosed; (b) there are no outstanding work orders, deficiency notices, or compliance orders affecting the Property; (c) the Property is not subject to any agreement of purchase and sale or option; (d) the Seller has the legal right and authority to sell the Property; and (e) the representations and warranties shall survive closing for a period of two (2) years.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Easement in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: The Grantor hereby grants to the Grantee, its successors and assigns, a perpetual and non-exclusive easement and right-of-way over, under, across, and upon the lands described in Schedule A for the purpose of access, ingress, and egress to and from the Dominant Tenement. The Grantee shall maintain the easement area in good repair and shall not interfere with the Grantor's reasonable use of the Servient Tenement. This easement shall run with the land and be binding upon successors in title.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Mortgage in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: The Chargor hereby charges the Property as security for the payment of the Principal Amount and interest thereon at the rate specified, payable in equal monthly installments of blended principal and interest. Upon default in any payment for a period of fifteen (15) days, the entire principal balance and accrued interest shall, at the option of the Chargee, become immediately due and payable. The Chargor shall maintain the Property in good repair, pay all taxes, and maintain fire insurance with the Chargee named as loss payee.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Construction Lien in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: The Owner shall retain a holdback of ten percent (10%) of the price of the services or materials as required under the Construction Act, RSO 1990, c C.30, section 22. The holdback shall be retained from each progress payment and released to the Contractor sixty (60) days after the publication of a certificate of substantial performance or completion, provided no liens have been preserved against the holdback within the prescribed time periods.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Settlement in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: The parties agree to settle and finally resolve all claims, counterclaims, and disputes between them arising out of or relating to the matters described in Schedule A (the 'Dispute'), on the terms set out in this Agreement. In consideration of the settlement amount and mutual releases, each party shall forthwith discontinue any and all proceedings commenced in connection with the Dispute, with each party bearing its own costs.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Release in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: Each party hereby releases and forever discharges the other party, together with its directors, officers, employees, agents, successors, and assigns, from any and all actions, causes of action, claims, demands, damages, losses, costs, and expenses of every nature and kind whatsoever, whether known or unknown, which any party ever had, now has, or may hereafter have arising out of or in connection with the subject matter of this Agreement. This release shall be binding upon the heirs, executors, administrators, and assigns of each party.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Release in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: In consideration of the severance package, the Employee releases and discharges the Employer from all claims arising from the employment relationship and its termination, including claims under the Employment Standards Act, 2000, the Human Rights Code, RSO 1990, c H.19, the Occupational Health and Safety Act, RSO 1990, c O.1, and the common law. The Employee confirms having had the opportunity to obtain independent legal advice and signing this Release voluntarily.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Confidentiality in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: The terms of this Settlement Agreement, including the settlement amount, are strictly confidential. Neither party shall disclose the terms of this Agreement to any third party except: (a) to their respective legal and financial advisors on a need-to-know basis; (b) as required by applicable law, regulation, or court order; or (c) as required for income tax reporting purposes. Any breach of this confidentiality provision shall entitle the non-breaching party to liquidated damages of $50,000.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Consent in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: The Defendant hereby consents to judgment being entered against it in the amount of [AMOUNT], inclusive of prejudgment interest and costs, in full and final satisfaction of the Plaintiff's claim in Court File No. [NUMBER]. This consent shall be filed with the Superior Court of Justice and the judgment may be enforced in accordance with the Courts of Justice Act, RSO 1990, c C.43, and the Rules of Civil Procedure.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Undertaking in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: The undersigned solicitor hereby undertakes to: (a) hold the documents and funds in trust pending completion of the transaction; (b) not release the funds until all conditions have been satisfied; (c) register the transfer/charge on title within two (2) business days of closing; and (d) report to the parties upon completion. This undertaking is given in accordance with the Rules of Professional Conduct of the Law Society of Ontario and is personally binding upon the undersigned solicitor.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Non Disparagement in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: Each party agrees not to make, publish, or cause to be made or published any statement, oral or written, that disparages, defames, or damages the reputation of the other party or its directors, officers, or employees. Nothing in this clause shall prevent either party from providing truthful testimony or information when required by law, regulation, or legal process.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Limitation Period in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: No action or proceeding arising out of or related to this Agreement may be commenced more than two (2) years after the cause of action is discovered or ought to have been discovered, in accordance with the Limitations Act, 2002, SO 2002, c 24, Schedule B. The parties agree that this limitation period is reasonable and shall apply notwithstanding any shorter or longer period that might otherwise apply.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Corporate Governance in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: BE IT RESOLVED THAT the Corporation is hereby authorized to enter into the Agreement described in the recitals hereto and to execute and deliver all documents and take all actions necessary or desirable to give effect thereto. The officers of the Corporation are hereby authorized and directed to execute the Agreement and all ancillary documents on behalf of the Corporation and to affix the corporate seal thereto.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Corporate Governance in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: The Corporation shall indemnify and hold harmless each director and officer of the Corporation from and against all costs, charges, and expenses, including legal fees, reasonably incurred in respect of any civil, criminal, or administrative action or proceeding to which the director or officer is made a party by reason of being a director or officer, provided they acted honestly and in good faith with a view to the best interests of the Corporation, as permitted by section 136 of the OBCA.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Corporate Governance in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: The shareholders of the Corporation hereby authorize the amendment of the articles of incorporation to: [describe amendment]. This special resolution has been approved by not less than two-thirds (2/3) of the votes cast by shareholders entitled to vote, in accordance with section 168 of the Business Corporations Act, RSO 1990, c B.16. The directors are authorized to file articles of amendment with the Ontario Ministry of Government and Consumer Services.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Service Agreement in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: The Service Provider shall provide the Services described in Schedule A in a professional and workmanlike manner, consistent with generally accepted industry standards. The Service Provider shall allocate sufficient qualified personnel and resources to perform the Services within the timelines agreed upon. Any changes to the scope of Services shall be documented in a written change order signed by both parties.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Service Agreement in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: If the Service Provider fails to meet the Service Levels set out in Schedule B, the Customer shall be entitled to service credits calculated as follows: for each 0.1% below the agreed availability target, a credit equal to 2% of the monthly service fee shall be applied to the next invoice. Service credits in any month shall not exceed 30% of the monthly fee. Service credits are the Customer's sole and exclusive remedy for failure to meet Service Levels.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Distribution Agreement in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: The Supplier hereby appoints the Distributor as its exclusive distributor for the Products in the Territory defined in Schedule A. During the term, the Supplier shall not sell the Products directly in the Territory or appoint any other distributor. The Distributor shall use commercially reasonable efforts to promote and sell the Products, maintain minimum annual purchase volumes as set out in Schedule B, and not sell products that compete directly with the Products.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Licensing Agreement in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: Subject to the terms of this Agreement and payment of the applicable fees, the Licensor hereby grants to the Licensee a non-exclusive, non-transferable, revocable licence to use the Software in object code form solely for the Licensee's internal business purposes. The Licensee shall not: (a) sublicense, sell, or distribute the Software; (b) modify, adapt, or create derivative works; (c) reverse-engineer, decompile, or disassemble the Software; or (d) use the Software for the benefit of any third party.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Data Processing in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: The Processor shall process Personal Data only on documented instructions from the Controller and solely for the purposes of performing the Services. The Processor shall: (a) implement appropriate technical and organizational security measures; (b) not engage sub-processors without prior written consent; (c) assist the Controller with data subject access requests within five (5) business days; (d) notify the Controller within 24 hours of discovering a personal data breach; and (e) return or delete all Personal Data upon termination.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Auto Renewal in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: This Agreement shall automatically renew for successive one (1) year periods upon expiry of the Initial Term unless either party provides written notice of non-renewal not less than ninety (90) days prior to the expiry of the then-current term. The fees for any renewal term shall be subject to adjustment by the Service Provider upon sixty (60) days' prior written notice, provided that any increase shall not exceed five percent (5%) of the fees in effect for the expiring term.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Exclusivity in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: During the Exclusivity Period of sixty (60) days commencing on the date of this letter of intent, the Vendor shall not, directly or indirectly: (a) solicit, encourage, or accept any proposal from any other person relating to the sale of the Business or substantially all of its assets; (b) provide any confidential information to any third party; or (c) enter into any agreement or understanding relating to the foregoing. The Vendor shall immediately notify the Purchaser if it receives any such proposal.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Payment Terms in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: All invoices are due and payable within thirty (30) days of the invoice date. Overdue amounts shall bear interest at the lesser of: (a) 1.5% per month (18% per annum), compounded monthly; or (b) the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, including section 347 of the Criminal Code, RSC 1985, c C-46. The Service Provider may suspend Services upon fifteen (15) days' written notice if any invoice remains unpaid for more than sixty (60) days.

instruction: Provide standard procurement clause language for Limitation Liability in CA-ON jurisdiction.
output: The aggregate liability of the Service Provider under this Agreement, whether in contract, tort (including negligence), or otherwise, shall not exceed the total fees paid by the Customer in the twelve (12) months preceding the event giving rise to the claim. This limitation shall not apply to: (a) the Service Provider's indemnification obligations; (b) breaches of confidentiality; (c) fraud or wilful misconduct; or (d) liability for death or personal injury caused by negligence.

instruction: What is the standard Canadian procurement clause for Standard Confidentiality Obligation?
output:
Each party (as 'Receiving Party') agrees to: (a) hold in strict confidence all Confidential Information of the other party (the 'Disclosing Party'); (b) not disclose Confidential Information to any third party without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party; and (c) use Confidential Information solely for the purposes of performing its obligations under this Contract. 'Confidential Information' means any information disclosed by a party that is designated as confidential or that reasonably should be understood to be confidential given the nature of the information and circumstances of disclosure, but excludes information that: (i) is or becomes publicly available without breach of this Contract; (ii) was rightfully known before disclosure; (iii) is rightfully obtained from a third party without restriction; or (iv) is independently developed.
Source: Custom / Best Practice
Notes: For Crown contracts, also reference the Security of Information Act, RSC 1985, c O-5 and applicable Treasury Board security standards.

instruction: What is the standard Canadian procurement clause for Termination for Convenience (Crown)?
output:

Upon receipt of such notice, the Contractor shall immediately cease work, except as necessary to preserve and protect work already performed. Canada's liability upon termination for convenience is limited to payment for work satisfactorily performed and accepted prior to the date of termination, together with reasonable and substantiated wind-down costs, not to exceed the unpaid Contract value.
Source: SACC Manual — Adapted (GC Std Clauses)
Notes: Standard in all Crown procurement contracts. No obligation to pay lost profits.

instruction: What is the standard Canadian procurement clause for Escalation and Arbitration?
output:
In the event of any dispute, controversy, or claim arising out of or relating to this Contract, or the breach, termination, or invalidity thereof, the parties shall first attempt to resolve the dispute through good-faith negotiation between senior representatives of each party for a period of thirty (30) calendar days from the date one party provides written notice of the dispute. If the dispute is not resolved through negotiation, either party may refer the matter to binding arbitration under the Arbitration Act, 1991, SO 1991, c 17 (Ontario), or the federal Commercial Arbitration Act, RSC 1985, c 17 (2nd Supp), as applicable. Nothing in this section prevents a party from seeking urgent injunctive or equitable relief from a court of competent jurisdiction.
Source: Custom / Best Practice
Notes: Crown contracts may not be arbitrable without statutory authority. Confirm with legal counsel.

instruction: Generate a complex procurement scenario involving a Software License dispute. A vendor has updated their Acceptable Use Policy mid-contract. Draft a 3-step reasoning chain: Issue Identification, Contractual Remedy, Drafted Response.
input: The Purchaser entered into a 3-year Master Service Agreement with a SaaS vendor. Section 12.3 of the MSA states: 'No amendment to this Agreement shall be valid unless set out in a formal written amendment executed by both parties.' The vendor unilaterally updated their Acceptable Use Policy to prohibit a use case that is core to the Purchaser's operations.
output:
{"issue_identification": "The vendor has breached Section 12.3 (Amendments) by unilaterally modifying the Acceptable Use Policy, which is incorporated by reference into the MSA. Under Canadian contract law, a unilateral change to material terms without consent constitutes a breach of contract. The change also potentially triggers the Material Adverse Change clause if it substantially impairs the Purchaser's intended use.", "contractual_remedy": "Section 12.3 requires mutual written consent for amendments. Section 18.1 (Termination for Default) permits the Purchaser to terminate if the vendor fails to cure a material breach within 15 business days of written notice. Section 14.2 (Dispute Resolution) requires escalation to senior management before formal proceedings. The Purchaser may also seek injunctive relief to prevent enforcement of the unilateral change.", "drafted_response": "NOTICE OF DISPUTE

To: [Vendor Legal Department]
From: [Purchaser]
Date: [Date]
Re: Unauthorized Amendment to Acceptable Use Policy — MSA Reference No. [XXX]

We write to formally notify you that your unilateral update to the Acceptable Use Policy dated [Date] constitutes a material breach of Section 12.3 of the Master Service Agreement executed between our organizations on [Date].

Section 12.3 expressly requires that amendments be set out in a formal written amendment executed by both parties. No such amendment was negotiated, agreed to, or executed.

We hereby demand that you: (1) immediately revert the Acceptable Use Policy to the version in effect at the date of the MSA; (2) confirm in writing within 15 business days that the prior version is reinstated; and (3) refrain from making further unilateral changes to any incorporated documents.

Failure to cure this breach within 15 business days may result in termination of the MSA pursuant to Section 18.1 and pursuit of all available remedies at law and in equity.

Yours truly,
[Purchaser Representative]"}

instruction: Analyze a construction procurement dispute where the contractor claims Force Majeure due to supply chain disruption. Identify the relevant clauses, assess the claim, and draft a response.
input: A federal construction contract for a $42M office renovation includes a Force Majeure clause (Section 9.1) that covers 'acts of God, war, terrorism, fire, flood, epidemic, pandemic, and governmental action.' The contractor claims a 6-month delay due to global steel supply shortages. The contract also includes a Liquidated Damages clause at 1% per week of delay, capped at 10%.
output:
{"issue_identification": "The contractor is claiming Force Majeure under Section 9.1 for supply chain disruption. However, general supply shortages and price increases are typically NOT Force Majeure events unless the clause specifically includes 'supply chain disruption' or 'shortage of materials.' The listed events (acts of God, war, etc.) are narrower. The contractor bears the burden of proving the event was beyond reasonable control and that mitigation efforts were undertaken.", "contractual_remedy": "Section 9.1 requires: (a) prompt written notice (within 5 business days); (b) evidence the delay was caused by a Force Majeure event; (c) demonstration of mitigation efforts. If the claim fails, Section 15.2 (Liquidated Damages) applies at 1% per week. For a 26-week delay on $42M, that is $4.2M capped at $4.2M (10% of $42M). The Purchaser should request: timeline of notice, evidence of steel sourcing attempts, and alternative supplier analysis.", "drafted_response": "RE: Force Majeure Claim — Contract No. [XXX]

We acknowledge receipt of your Force Majeure claim dated [Date]. After review, we note the following concerns:

1. Section 9.1 does not specifically enumerate supply chain disruption or material shortages as Force Majeure events.
2. Your notice was provided [X] days after the alleged triggering event, exceeding the 5-business-day notice requirement.
3. No evidence of mitigation efforts (alternative suppliers, material substitution) has been provided.

Please provide within 10 business days: (a) documentary evidence that steel was unavailable from all reasonable sources; (b) a log of alternative suppliers contacted; (c) a revised project schedule showing the critical path impact.

Pending review of this evidence, we reserve all rights under Section 15.2 (Liquidated Damages)."}

instruction: A SaaS vendor suffered a data breach affecting the Purchaser's customer data. Analyze the indemnification and data breach notification obligations.
input: The MSA includes: Section 8.1 (Data Breach Notification) requiring 24-hour notice. Section 11.3 (Indemnification) states the vendor shall indemnify the Purchaser for losses arising from vendor's breach of data protection obligations. Section 13.1 (Limitation of Liability) caps aggregate liability at 12 months' fees ($240,000). Section 8.4 requires the vendor to bear costs of breach response including notification and credit monitoring. The vendor notified the Purchaser 72 hours after discovering the breach.
output:
{"issue_identification": "Two breaches: (1) Late notification — Section 8.1 requires 24-hour notice but vendor took 72 hours, a 48-hour delay. (2) The underlying data breach itself triggers indemnification under Section 11.3. Under PIPEDA and Ontario PHIPA, the Purchaser may have independent notification obligations to affected individuals. The late vendor notice has shortened the Purchaser's response window.", "contractual_remedy": "Section 11.3 indemnification covers: breach response costs, notification to affected individuals, credit monitoring, forensic investigation, and regulatory fines. Section 8.4 explicitly allocates these costs to the vendor. However, Section 13.1 caps aggregate liability at $240,000 (12 months' fees). Key question: does the liability cap carve out data breach indemnification? If Section 13.4 (Carve-Outs) excludes data protection breaches from the cap, full recovery is available. If not, recovery is limited to $240,000.", "drafted_response": "NOTICE OF INDEMNIFICATION CLAIM

We hereby provide formal notice of an indemnification claim under Section 11.3 of the MSA.

The data breach disclosed on [Date] has resulted in the following losses to date:
- Forensic investigation costs: $85,000
- Legal counsel fees: $45,000
- Customer notification costs (estimated): $120,000
- Credit monitoring services (estimated): $200,000
- Regulatory response costs: $30,000

We note that your notification was provided 72 hours after discovery, in violation of the 24-hour requirement in Section 8.1. This delay has materially impacted our ability to mitigate harm.

Please confirm within 5 business days that you accept the indemnification obligation and provide your insurer's contact information for direct claims coordination."}

instruction: A losing bidder believes the procurement evaluation was unfair. Analyze whether they have grounds for a CITT complaint and draft the complaint framework.
input: A federal IT services procurement used 70% technical / 30% price evaluation. The losing bidder scored 72% technical (above the 70% threshold) but was ranked second. The winning bidder's technical score was 68% but their price was 40% lower. The RFP stated the minimum technical score was 70%. The losing bidder believes the winning bidder should have been disqualified for failing to meet the minimum technical threshold.
output:
{"issue_identification": "If the winning bidder scored 68% technical against a stated minimum threshold of 70%, they should have been screened out before price evaluation. Awarding to a bidder that failed mandatory requirements violates CFTA Article 504 (non-discrimination and transparency) and the evaluation methodology published in the RFP. This is a strong ground for a CITT complaint.", "contractual_remedy": "Under the CITT Procurement Inquiry Regulations (SOR/93-602), the complainant must file within 10 working days of learning the basis for the complaint. The CITT can recommend: (a) re-evaluation excluding the non-compliant bid; (b) contract termination and re-tender; or (c) compensation for bid preparation costs or lost profits. Given that the complainant met all mandatory requirements and the winner did not, re-evaluation is the most likely remedy.", "drafted_response": "COMPLAINT TO THE CANADIAN INTERNATIONAL TRADE TRIBUNAL

Complainant: [Company Name]
Procurement Reference: [Solicitation No.]
Contracting Department: [Department]

GROUNDS FOR COMPLAINT:

1. The RFP (Section 4.2) established a mandatory minimum technical score of 70%.
2. The successful bidder received a technical score of 68%, below the mandatory threshold.
3. Despite failing to meet mandatory requirements, the successful bidder was not disqualified and was awarded the contract.
4. This evaluation is inconsistent with the published evaluation criteria and violates CFTA Article 504.

RELIEF SOUGHT:
(a) A determination that the procurement was not conducted in accordance with CFTA Chapter 5;
(b) Re-evaluation of bids with the non-compliant bid excluded;
(c) Award of the contract to the Complainant; or
(d) In the alternative, compensation for bid preparation costs of $[Amount] and lost profits of $[Amount].

This complaint is filed within 10 working days of the Complainant becoming aware of the evaluation results."}

instruction: A cloud service provider failed to meet the 99.5% uptime SLA for three consecutive months. Analyze the contractual remedies and draft a notice.
input: The MSA specifies 99.5% monthly availability. Actual uptime was: Month 1: 98.2%, Month 2: 97.8%, Month 3: 99.1%. Service credits are 2% of monthly fees per 0.1% below target. Monthly fee is $50,000. Section 18.3 allows termination if SLA is missed for three consecutive months.
output:
{"issue_identification": "The vendor has failed the 99.5% uptime SLA for three consecutive months. Service credit calculations: Month 1: (99.5-98.2)/0.1 x 2% = 26% of $50,000 = $13,000. Month 2: (99.5-97.8)/0.1 x 2% = 34% of $50,000 = $17,000 (likely capped at 30% = $15,000). Month 3: (99.5-99.1)/0.1 x 2% = 8% of $50,000 = $4,000. Total credits: $32,000. Additionally, Section 18.3 termination right is now triggered.", "contractual_remedy": "The Purchaser has two options: (1) Claim service credits of $32,000 and require a remediation plan with binding improvement targets; (2) Exercise the termination right under Section 18.3 due to three consecutive months of SLA failure. If terminating, the Purchaser is entitled to transition services (Section 20.1) for up to 90 days and must invoke data portability rights.", "drafted_response": "NOTICE OF SLA BREACH AND TERMINATION RIGHT

This letter serves as formal notice that your service has failed to meet the 99.5% availability SLA for three consecutive months:
- [Month 1]: 98.2% (1.3% below SLA)
- [Month 2]: 97.8% (1.7% below SLA)
- [Month 3]: 99.1% (0.4% below SLA)

Service credits totalling $32,000 are due immediately.

Pursuant to Section 18.3, we hereby reserve our right to terminate this Agreement. Before exercising this right, we require within 10 business days: (1) root cause analysis for each outage; (2) a binding remediation plan with monthly improvement targets; (3) confirmation of service credits.

Failure to provide a satisfactory remediation plan will result in termination with 30 days notice."}

instruction: An IT contractor claims ownership of software code developed under a federal agile development contract. Analyze the IP provisions and draft a Crown assertion of ownership.
input: A federal agile development contract (Section 7.1) states all Foreground IP vests in Canada upon creation. The contractor embedded a proprietary framework (Background IP) into the deliverables without disclosure. Section 7.3 requires the contractor to disclose all Background IP in Schedule D. Schedule D was left blank. The contractor now claims the entire codebase is their IP because it is built on their proprietary framework.
output:
{"issue_identification": "Two issues: (1) Section 7.1 clearly vests all Foreground IP in Canada. Code written during the contract is Foreground IP regardless of what framework it uses. (2) The contractor breached Section 7.3 by failing to disclose the proprietary framework in Schedule D. By incorporating undisclosed Background IP, the contractor has created an entanglement that does not change Canada's ownership of Foreground IP but may complicate extraction.", "contractual_remedy": "Canada owns the Foreground IP under Section 7.1. The contractor's failure to disclose Background IP in Schedule D is a material breach. Under Section 7.4, where Background IP is incorporated into deliverables, the contractor grants Canada a non-exclusive, irrevocable, royalty-free licence. Canada should: (a) assert Foreground IP ownership; (b) demand the Background IP licence under Section 7.4; (c) require the contractor to provide documentation enabling Canada to use the deliverables independently; (d) consider requiring the contractor to refactor to remove proprietary dependencies.", "drafted_response": "RE: Assertion of Crown Intellectual Property Rights — Contract No. [XXX]

Canada hereby asserts its ownership of all Foreground Intellectual Property created under this Contract pursuant to Section 7.1.

We note that your proprietary framework was incorporated into the deliverables without disclosure in Schedule D, in breach of Section 7.3. This non-disclosure does not alter Canada's IP rights. Pursuant to Section 7.4, you have granted Canada a non-exclusive, irrevocable, royalty-free licence to all Background IP incorporated into the deliverables.

You are directed to: (1) provide complete source code, build scripts, and documentation within 10 business days; (2) identify all Background IP components in writing; (3) confirm the licence grant under Section 7.4; (4) provide a refactoring plan to reduce proprietary dependencies.

Failure to comply may result in termination for default under Section 18.1."}

instruction: Compare vendor-friendly and customer-friendly versions of a Limitation of Liability clause. Explain the differences and negotiation leverage.
input: Limitation of Liability clause for a $500K SaaS contract
response: {"vendor_friendly": "The Provider's aggregate liability shall not exceed the fees paid in the three (3) months preceding the claim. In no event shall the Provider be liable for indirect, incidental, consequential, or punitive damages, including loss of data, revenue, or profits.", "customer_friendly": "The Provider's aggregate liability shall not exceed two times (2x) the total annual fees paid or payable. The limitation on consequential damages shall not apply to: (a) data breaches; (b) IP indemnification; (c) fraud or wilful misconduct; (d) breaches of confidentiality. The Provider shall maintain cyber liability insurance of not less than $2,000,000.", "key_differences": ["Cap: 3 months fees vs 2x annual fees (8x difference)", "Consequential damages: blanket exclusion vs carved-out exceptions", "No insurance requirement vs $2M cyber liability mandate", "No data breach accountability vs explicit data breach carve-out"], "negotiation_tip": "Start with 2x annual fees and negotiate down. Never accept a cap below 1x annual fees. Always carve out data breaches and IP indemnification from the consequential damages exclusion."}

instruction: Compare vendor-friendly and customer-friendly versions of an Indemnification clause.
input: Indemnification clause for an IT consulting engagement
response: {"vendor_friendly": "The Consultant shall indemnify the Client solely for direct damages arising from the Consultant's gross negligence or wilful misconduct in performing the Services. The Consultant's indemnification obligation shall not exceed the fees paid under this Agreement. The Client shall provide prompt notice and sole control of the defence.", "customer_friendly": "The Consultant shall defend, indemnify, and hold harmless the Client from all claims, damages, losses, and expenses (including reasonable legal fees) arising from: (a) any breach of this Agreement; (b) any negligent act or omission; (c) any IP infringement; (d) any breach of applicable law. The Consultant shall maintain professional liability insurance of $2,000,000 to support this obligation. The indemnification obligation shall survive termination for 3 years.", "key_differences": ["Trigger: gross negligence only vs any negligence or breach", "Scope: direct damages only vs all claims including legal fees", "Cap: fee-capped vs uncapped (backed by insurance)", "IP coverage: excluded vs explicitly included", "Survival: silent vs 3-year survival period"], "negotiation_tip": "Push for 'negligence' not just 'gross negligence' — the bar for gross negligence is very high in Canadian law. Always require insurance backing for the indemnity."}

instruction: Compare vendor-friendly and customer-friendly versions of a Termination for Convenience clause.
input: Termination for Convenience in a 3-year managed services contract
response: {"vendor_friendly": "Either party may terminate this Agreement for convenience upon 180 days' written notice. Upon termination by the Client for convenience, the Client shall pay: (a) all fees for services rendered; (b) a termination fee equal to 50% of the remaining contract value; (c) all demobilization and transition costs.", "customer_friendly": "The Client may terminate this Agreement for convenience upon 30 days' written notice. Upon termination, the Client's liability is limited to payment for services satisfactorily performed and accepted prior to the termination date, together with reasonable and substantiated wind-down costs not exceeding 10% of the remaining contract value. No termination fee shall apply.", "key_differences": ["Notice period: 180 days vs 30 days", "Termination fee: 50% of remaining value vs no fee", "Wind-down costs: unlimited vs capped at 10%", "Mutuality: bilateral vs Client-only right"], "negotiation_tip": "In Crown procurement, termination for convenience is a standard Crown right with no termination fee — this is non-negotiable in SACC contracts. For commercial contracts, negotiate the termination fee down from 50% to 0-15% of remaining value."}

instruction: Compare vendor-friendly and customer-friendly versions of a Data Residency clause for Canadian government contracts.
input: Data Residency clause for a cloud services contract with Protected B data
response: {"vendor_friendly": "The Provider shall use commercially reasonable efforts to store data within Canada. The Provider may process data outside Canada for disaster recovery, maintenance, or support purposes provided appropriate safeguards are in place. The Provider shall comply with applicable data protection laws.", "customer_friendly": "All data, including personal information, Protected B information, backups, logs, and metadata, shall be stored, processed, transmitted, and backed up exclusively within the geographic boundaries of Canada at all times. No data shall leave Canada for any purpose including disaster recovery, support, or maintenance without the prior written consent of Canada. The Provider shall certify the physical location of all data centres used and permit Canada to audit compliance. Any unauthorized transfer outside Canada shall constitute a material breach.", "key_differences": ["Scope: commercially reasonable efforts vs absolute requirement", "Exceptions: DR and support carve-outs vs no exceptions without consent", "Coverage: data only vs data, backups, logs, metadata", "Audit: no audit right vs certification and audit rights", "Breach: no consequence vs material breach for violation"], "negotiation_tip": "For Protected B data, there is no flexibility — data MUST stay in Canada per TBS Directive. Do not accept 'commercially reasonable efforts' language. Require certification of data centre locations."}

instruction: Compare vendor-friendly and customer-friendly versions of a Force Majeure clause post-COVID.
input: Force Majeure clause for a supply chain contract
response: {"vendor_friendly": "Neither party shall be liable for delays caused by events beyond reasonable control, including but not limited to pandemic, epidemic, supply chain disruption, labour shortages, raw material shortages, transportation delays, government regulations, and any other cause that could not have been reasonably foreseen. The affected party shall be entitled to an equitable extension of time and price adjustment.", "customer_friendly": "Force Majeure events are limited to: acts of God, war, terrorism, fire, flood, earthquake, and government-ordered shutdowns. Pandemic and supply chain disruption are expressly excluded as foreseeable risks that the Supplier is expected to mitigate through business continuity planning. The Supplier shall maintain 90 days of safety stock. If a Force Majeure event continues for more than 60 days, the Purchaser may terminate without penalty.", "key_differences": ["Scope: expansive (including supply chain) vs narrow (acts of God only)", "Pandemic: included vs expressly excluded as foreseeable", "Price adjustment: automatic vs no adjustment", "Mitigation: no requirement vs 90-day safety stock mandate", "Termination: no right vs 60-day termination trigger"], "negotiation_tip": "Post-COVID, vendors will push to include pandemic and supply chain disruption. Resist this — these are now foreseeable risks. Instead, require business continuity plans and safety stock. Accept pandemic only if paired with a termination right after 60-90 days."}

instruction: Define these insurance terms in the context of Canadian government procurement. Return JSON with each term's definition and practical significance.
input: Additional Insured, Waiver of Subrogation, Cross-Liability, Certificate of Insurance, Occurrence vs Claims-Made
output: {"definitions": [{"term": "Additional Insured", "definition": "A party added to the Contractor's insurance policy who receives coverage under that policy. In procurement, Canada (the Crown) is named as Additional Insured on the Contractor's CGL policy.", "practical_significance": "If a third party sues Canada for something the Contractor caused, the Contractor's insurer must defend and indemnify Canada. Without this, Canada would need to claim against the Contractor directly."}, {"term": "Waiver of Subrogation", "definition": "The insurer waives its right to recover (subrogate) against the named party after paying a claim. In procurement, the Contractor's insurer waives subrogation rights against Canada.", "practical_significance": "Prevents the Contractor's insurer from suing Canada to recover claim payments. Common on Builders Risk policies where both parties have insurable interest."}, {"term": "Cross-Liability", "definition": "A clause treating each insured party as if they had a separate policy. One Additional Insured can claim against another under the same policy.", "practical_significance": "If Canada is injured by the Contractor's negligence, Canada can claim under the Contractor's CGL even though both are named insureds on the same policy."}, {"term": "Certificate of Insurance (COI)", "definition": "A document issued by the insurer confirming the types of coverage, limits, effective dates, and named insureds. It is evidence of insurance but does NOT confer coverage.", "practical_significance": "Canada requires COIs within 10 business days of contract award as proof of compliance. Always verify the COI matches the contract requirements — check limits, Additional Insured status, and policy dates."}, {"term": "Occurrence vs Claims-Made", "definition": "Occurrence: covers events that happen during the policy period regardless of when the claim is filed. Claims-Made: covers claims filed during the policy period regardless of when the event occurred. Claims-Made requires a retroactive date and extended reporting period (tail).", "practical_significance": "CGL is typically occurrence-based. Professional Liability (E&O) and Cyber are often claims-made. For claims-made policies, always require: (a) retroactive date no later than contract start; (b) tail coverage of 2+ years after contract end."}]}

instruction: List the top 10 insurance red flags an sLM should detect when reviewing vendor insurance submissions for Canadian government procurement.
output: {"red_flags": [{"rank": 1, "flag": "Aggregate-Only Limits (no Per Occurrence)", "why": "A $2M aggregate shared across all the vendor's clients could be exhausted before the Purchaser needs it. The Purchaser would have to sue the vendor, then the vendor claims on insurance — adding months of delay.", "check": "COI must explicitly name the Purchaser as Additional Insured on CGL"}, {"rank": 3, "flag": "Lapsed or Expired Policy Dates", "why": "A COI with an expiry date before the contract start date means zero coverage from day one.", "check": "Policy period must encompass the entire contract term including renewals"}, {"rank": 4, "flag": "Excessive Deductibles", "why": "A $100K+ deductible means the vendor pays out-of-pocket before insurance responds. Common with US-based vendors whose policies exclude Canadian operations.", "check": "Verify policy territory includes all project locations"}, {"rank": 6, "flag": "Claims-Made CGL (should be Occurrence)", "why": "Claims-made CGL only covers claims filed during the policy period. If the policy lapses, past incidents become uninsured. Occurrence-based covers incidents that happen during the policy period regardless of when the claim is filed.", "check": "CGL must be occurrence-based. If claims-made, require 3+ year tail"}, {"rank": 7, "flag": "Cyber Exclusion in E&O Policy", "why": "A tech vendor whose E&O policy specifically excludes data breaches, cyber attacks, or network security incidents. This is the #1 risk for IT contracts.", "check": "For IT contracts, verify E&O does not exclude cyber events — or require separate Cyber Liability policy"}, {"rank": 8, "flag": "Insurer Below A- VII AM Best Rating", "why": "A financially weak insurer may not be able to pay claims. This defeats the purpose of requiring the vendor to carry insurance.", "check": "Require Waiver of Subrogation in favour of the Purchaser on all policies"}, {"rank": 10, "flag": "Insufficient Notice of Cancellation", "why": "If the vendor's policy is cancelled with only 10 days notice, the Purchaser has no time to react.
